trace.detail: 7 grounded contradictions (2 dropped by the 2-source grounding guardrail)

conflicts.title: Password rotation: every 90 days vs never expires
conflicts.explanation: The security policy mandates rotation every 90 days, while onboarding tells new hires their password never expires. Both cannot be true.

side_a.statement: All user account passwords must be rotated at least once every 90 days.

side_b.statement: Set your account password once during onboarding; your password never expires.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: Information Security Policy (precedence 5) outranks New Hire Onboarding Guide (precedence 2).

conflicts.title: MFA: required vs optional
conflicts.explanation: Security requires MFA for every employee without exception; onboarding says it is optional during the first week.

side_a.statement: Multi-factor authentication is required for every employee accessing internal systems, without exception.

side_b.statement: MFA is optional during your first week while you get set up.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: Information Security Policy (precedence 5) outranks New Hire Onboarding Guide (precedence 2).

conflicts.title: API authentication: X-Api-Key vs Bearer token
conflicts.explanation: The v1 reference authenticates via the X-Api-Key header, but the v2 changelog replaced it with Authorization: Bearer and stopped accepting X-Api-Key.

side_a.statement: Authenticate by sending your API key in the X-Api-Key request header.

side_b.statement: Authentication now uses the Authorization: Bearer <key> header; the legacy X-Api-Key header is no longer accepted.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: API Changelog (v2) (precedence 4) outranks Public API Reference (v1) (precedence 3).

conflicts.title: Primary DB lock handling: restart vs never restart
conflicts.explanation: The runbook says to restart the primary database to clear lock contention; the architecture reference forbids that exact action because it risks data loss, and prescribes failover instead.

side_a.statement: If you observe lock contention on the primary database, restart the primary instance to clear the stuck locks.

side_b.statement: Never restart the primary database to resolve lock contention; fail over to a healthy replica instead.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: Platform Architecture Reference (precedence 4) outranks Database Operations Runbook (precedence 3).

conflicts.title: Uptime SLA: 99.9% vs 99.5%
conflicts.explanation: The public pricing sheet guarantees 99.9% uptime, but the signed Master Services Agreement commits to 99.5%. The customer-facing promise exceeds the contractual commitment.

side_a.statement: We guarantee 99.9% monthly uptime for all paid plans.

side_b.statement: The Provider commits to 99.5% monthly uptime.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: Master Services Agreement (precedence 6) outranks Public Pricing Sheet (precedence 3).

conflicts.title: API rate limit: 100 rps vs 60 rps
conflicts.explanation: The v1 reference advertises 100 requests/second per key; the v2 changelog reduced it to 60 as a breaking change.

side_a.statement: Each API key is allowed up to 100 requests per second.

side_b.statement: The per-key rate limit is now 60 requests per second, reduced from the previous 100.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: API Changelog (v2) (precedence 4) outranks Public API Reference (v1) (precedence 3).

conflicts.title: Refund window: 30 days vs 14 days
conflicts.explanation: The pricing sheet offers a 30-day refund window, while the Master Services Agreement limits refunds to 14 days from the invoice date.

side_a.statement: Customers on the Standard plan may request a full refund within 30 days of purchase.

side_b.statement: The Customer may request a refund within 14 days of the invoice date.

conflicts.resolution_basis: Authority: Master Services Agreement (precedence 6) outranks Public Pricing Sheet (precedence 3).
